title: Alnus glutinosa lusus incisa
intro: Alnus glutinosa lusus incisa, commonly known as the cut-leaved alder, is a shrub or small tree belonging to the birch family (Betulaceae). The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, making it useful in treating various conditions such as wounds, infections, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Alnus glutinosa lusus incisa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often used to prepare infusions, tinctures, and decoctions, which are used to treat colds, fever, and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Alnus glutinosa lusus incisa is a variety of the black alder tree, specifically within the family Betulaceae. It is classified within the genus Alnus, and the species Alnus glutinosa. This variety is further classified as a type of flowering plant, with the kingdom being Plantae. The classification is as follows: kingdom Plantae, family Betulaceae, genus Alnus, species Alnus glutinosa, variety lusus incisa. This classification helps to identify and distinguish Alnus glutinosa lusus incisa from other plant species.

properties: Alnus glutinosa lusus incisa, also known as incised alder, has been used in traditional medicine for its various therapeutic properties. The plant's bark and leaves have been reported to exhibit anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities. Its extracts have been studied for their potential in treating wounds, ulcers, and skin conditions due to their ability to promote tissue repair and reduce inflammation.
